name: Buster Cooper
abstract: George "Buster" Cooper (April 4, 1929 – May 13, 2016) was an American jazz trombonist.

works.t: Things Ain't What They Used To Be
Second Sacred Concert: The Shepherd (Who Watches Over the Night Flock)

concerts.label: Duke Ellington in His Concert of Sacred Music

about_quotes.speaker: Arnett Cobb
about_quotes.evidence: Milton Buckner, Andre Preppy Presciano on piano, Roland le Bourgeois on bass, Panama Francis on drums, Wallace Davenport on trumpet, Buster Cooper on trombone. ... and yours truly, Arnette Cobb.

about_quotes.speaker: Lawrence Brown
about_quotes.evidence: Then in going on through the sixties, the trombone section was pretty stable there. It was you, and Chuck Connors, and Buster Cooper steadily through -- almost through the end of '69. Yes.

about_quotes.speaker: Monk Montgomery
about_quotes.evidence: The trombone section was Jimmy Cleveland, Buster Cooper and Al -- oh, I'm sorry, I can't think of that trombone player's name (Hayes).

about_quotes.speaker: Frank Capp
about_quotes.evidence: Buster Cooper was until he just moved to Florida. So we have kept a pretty consistent personnel in our orchestra.
about_quotes.doc_title: Frank Capp interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, September 3, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Plas Johnson
about_quotes.evidence: Yeah I was with the first band... Buster Cooper, Benny Powell. It was fun playing.
about_quotes.doc_title: Plas Johnson interviewed by Monk Rowe, Sarasota, Florida, April 12, 1996

about_quotes.speaker: Johnny Varro
about_quotes.evidence: John Lamb I played with here with Buster Cooper the other day.
about_quotes.doc_title: Johnny Varro interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clearwater Beach, Florida, March 16, 2001
